[docs/specification.mdx](docs/specification.mdx)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThe **Skill Creation Guide** provides a comprehensive framework for developing, optimizing, and validating Agent Skills. This workflow ensures that skills are not only technically functional but also highly discoverable and reliable for AI agents.\n\n## Workflow Overview\n\nThe skill creation process follows a lifecycle from initial scoping to automated evaluation. The repository provides a set of conventions and tools to support this journey.\n\n### 1. Definition and Discovery\nSkills are defined as a directory containing a `SKILL.md` file [docs/specification.mdx:8-12](). This file uses YAML frontmatter to define metadata like `name` and `description` [docs/specification.mdx:21-32](), which agents use for **Discovery** at session startup [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:65-65]().\n\n### 2. Implementation\nThe Markdown body of the `SKILL.md` provides the instructions the agent follows once a skill is activated [docs/specification.mdx:176-178](). Complex logic can be offloaded to executable scripts within a `scripts/` directory [docs/specification.mdx:189-192]().\n\n### 3. Iteration and Optimization\nAuthors refine skills by optimizing descriptions to improve **Trigger Rates** [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:87-89]() and applying best practices such as **Progressive Disclosure** to manage context window limits [docs/specification.mdx:214-222]().\n\n### 4. Evaluation\nHigh-quality skills are verified using an evaluation framework defined in `evals/evals.json` [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:17-19](), which supports assertion-based grading and baseline comparisons [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:48-50]().\n\n---\n\n## Skill Architecture: From Intent to Execution\n\nThe following diagrams illustrate how natural language user intent is mapped to code entities and filesystem structures during the skill lifecycle.\n\n### Discovery and Activation Flow\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    UserIntent[\"'Roll a d20' (Natural Language)\"] --\u003e AgentScanner[\"Agent Discovery Logic\"]\n    \n    subgraph \"Code Entity Space: .agents/skills/\"\n        SkillDir[\"roll-dice/ directory\"]\n        SkillMD[\"SKILL.md\"]\n        Frontmatter[\"YAML Frontmatter (name, description)\"]\n        MarkdownBody[\"Markdown Instructions (echo $((RANDOM % sides + 1)))\"]\n    end\n\n    AgentScanner --\u003e Frontmatter\n    Frontmatter -- \"Match Found\" --\u003e Activation[\"Skill Activation\"]\n    Activation --\u003e MarkdownBody\n    MarkdownBody -- \"Execute\" --\u003e Shell[\"Bash/PowerShell Runtime\"]\n```\nSources: [docs/specification.mdx:8-32](), [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-45](), [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:61-71]()\n\n### Scripting and Resource Resolution\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Natural Language Space\"\n        Instruction[\"'Run the validation script'\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Code Entity Space: Skill Directory\"\n        SKILL_MD[\"SKILL.md\"]\n        ScriptsDir[\"scripts/\"]\n        ProcessPy[\"process.py (PEP 723)\"]\n        ValidateSh[\"validate.sh\"]\n    end\n\n    Instruction --\u003e SKILL_MD\n    SKILL_MD -- \"Relative Path Resolution\" --\u003e ScriptsDir\n    ScriptsDir --\u003e ProcessPy\n    ScriptsDir --\u003e ValidateSh\n    ProcessPy -- \"uv run\" --\u003e PythonEnv[\"Isolated Python Environment\"]\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:89-116](), [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:129-149]()\n\n---\n\n## Guide Sections\n\n### [Quickstart](#4.1)\nLearn how to create a basic \"Roll Dice\" skill. This section covers the minimal directory setup, required `SKILL.md` fields, and how to verify the skill in VS Code using GitHub Copilot.\n*For details, see [Quickstart](#4.1).*\n\n### [Best Practices](#4.2)\nMaximize skill quality by focusing on domain-specific expertise rather than generic LLM knowledge. Topics include context parsimony (omitting what the agent already knows), designing coherent units, and matching instruction specificity to task fragility.\n*For details, see [Best Practices](#4.2).*\n\n### [Optimizing Descriptions](#4.3)\nThe `description` field is the single point of failure for skill discovery. This guide explains how to use imperative phrasing, design \"near-miss\" test cases, and calculate trigger rates to ensure your skill activates only when appropriate.\n*For details, see [Optimizing Descriptions](#4.3).*\n\n### [Evaluating Skills](#4.4)\nMove beyond \"vibe-based\" testing with a structured evaluation framework. Learn how to use `evals/evals.json` to define test cases, run \"with-skill\" vs \"baseline\" comparisons, and implement assertion-based grading.\n*For details, see [Evaluating Skills](#4.4).*\n\n### [Using Scripts](#4.5)\nExtend skill capabilities with executable code. This section covers one-off commands (using `uvx`, `npx`), self-contained scripts with inline dependencies (PEP 723 for Python, Deno, Bun), and best practices for agentic script design.\n*For details, see [Using Scripts](#4.5).*\n\n---\n\n## Summary of Skill Creation Components\n\n| Component | File/Location | Purpose |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Metadata** | `SKILL.md` (Frontmatter) | Controls discovery and triggering [docs/specification.mdx:23-32]() |\n| **Instructions** | `SKILL.md` (Body) | Provides the logic for the agent to follow [docs/specification.mdx:176-178]() |\n| **Scripts** | `scripts/` | Bundles reusable, complex logic [docs/specification.mdx:189-192]() |\n| **Evaluations** | `evals/evals.json` | Defines test cases and success criteria [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:17-19]() |\n| **References** | `references/` | Large-scale documentation for on-demand loading [docs/specification.mdx:198-205]() |\n\nSources: [docs/specification.mdx:8-205](), [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-45](), [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:7-7](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:17-19]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"23:T197a,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Quickstart\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/clients.mdx](docs/clients.mdx)\n- [docs/docs.json](docs/docs.json)\n- [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx](docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx](docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx](docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx)\n- [docs/snippets/ClientShowcase.jsx](docs/snippets/ClientShowcase.jsx)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis guide provides a step-by-step walkthrough for creating your first Agent Skill. You will build a \"Dice Roller\" skill that enables an AI agent to generate random numbers using terminal commands. By the end of this guide, you will understand how to structure a skill directory, write the `SKILL.md` file, and verify its discovery by a compatible agent.\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nTo follow this guide, you should have a compatible agent environment. While Agent Skills is an open standard, the following tools are recommended for initial verification:\n*   **VS Code** with the **GitHub Copilot** extension [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:10-10]().\n*   Access to **Agent Mode** within the Copilot Chat panel [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:51-51]().\n\n## 1. Directory Setup\n\nAn Agent Skill is defined as a directory containing a mandatory `SKILL.md` file [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-18](). Agents typically scan specific paths to discover these skills.\n\n1.  Navigate to your project root.\n2.  Create the standard discovery directory: `.agents/skills/`.\n3.  Create a unique subdirectory for your skill: `roll-dice/`.\n4.  Create the specification file: `SKILL.md`.\n\n**Target Path:** `.agents/skills/roll-dice/SKILL.md` [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-18]().\n\n## 2. Writing the SKILL.md\n\nThe `SKILL.md` file consists of two primary sections: YAML frontmatter for discovery and a Markdown body for execution instructions.\n\n### Frontmatter (Discovery Metadata)\nThe frontmatter tells the agent *what* the skill is and *when* to use it.\n*   **`name`**: A short identifier for the skill. It must match the folder name [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:43-43]().\n*   **`description`**: The trigger logic. The agent uses this to decide whether to activate the skill based on the user's prompt [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:44-44]().\n\n### Body (Execution Instructions)\nThe body contains the specific procedures, code snippets, or commands the agent should follow once the skill is activated [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:45-45]().\n\n### Implementation Example\nCopy the following content into your `.agents/skills/roll-dice/SKILL.md`:\n\n```markdown\n---\nname: roll-dice\ndescription: Roll dice using a random number generator. Use when asked to roll a die (d6, d20, etc.), roll dice, or generate a random dice roll.\n---\n\nTo roll a die, use the following command that generates a random number from 1\nto the given number of sides:\n\n```bash\necho $((RANDOM % \u003csides\u003e + 1))\n```\n\n```powershell\nGet-Random -Minimum 1 -Maximum (\u003csides\u003e + 1)\n```\n\nReplace `\u003csides\u003e` with the number of sides on the die (e.g., 6 for a standard\ndie, 20 for a d20).\n```\n[docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:20-39]()\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-45]()\n\n## 3. Data Flow and Progressive Disclosure\n\nAgent Skills utilize a **Progressive Disclosure** model to manage the agent's context window efficiently [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:71-71](). Instead of loading every instruction for every skill at once, the agent follows a three-step lifecycle.\n\n### Skill Lifecycle Diagram\nThe following diagram illustrates the transition from Natural Language triggers to the execution of code entities (scripts/commands).\n\n**Skill Activation Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Natural Language Space\"\n        UserPrompt[\"User: 'Roll a d20'\"]\n        Match[\"Agent matches Prompt to SKILL.md 'description'\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Code Entity Space\"\n        Discovery[\"Agent scans .agents/skills/\"]\n        Metadata[\"Read SKILL.md Frontmatter (name, description)\"]\n        Activation[\"Load full SKILL.md Body into Context\"]\n        Execution[\"Execute Bash/PowerShell Command\"]\n    end\n\n    UserPrompt --\u003e Discovery\n    Discovery --\u003e Metadata\n    Metadata --\u003e Match\n    Match --\u003e Activation\n    Activation --\u003e Execution\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:61-71]()\n\n## 4. Verification\n\nTo verify that the agent has correctly indexed your new skill:\n\n1.  **Check Discovery**: In your agent's chat interface (e.g., VS Code Copilot Chat in Agent Mode), type `/skills` [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:52-52]().\n2.  **Confirm Visibility**: Ensure `roll-dice` appears in the list of available skills.\n3.  **Test Triggering**: Send a prompt such as **\"Roll a d20\"** [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:53-53]().\n4.  **Observe Execution**: The agent should identify the skill via the description, load the instructions, and attempt to run the terminal command (e.g., `echo $((RANDOM % 20 + 1))`) [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:55-55]().\n\n### Verification Logic Diagram\nThis diagram bridges the user's intent to the specific file system entities that satisfy the request.\n\n**Entity Mapping**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Natural Language Intent\"\n        Intent[\"'I need a random number'\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"File System Entities\"\n        Folder[\".agents/skills/roll-dice/\"]\n        File[\"SKILL.md\"]\n        Logic[\"Bash/PowerShell Code Block\"]\n    end\n\n    Intent -- \"Triggers via Description\" --\u003e File\n    File -- \"Contained in\" --\u003e Folder\n    File -- \"Defines\" --\u003e Logic\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-45](), [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:61-71]()\n\n## 5. Summary Table\n\n| Component | Purpose | Location/Requirement |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Skill Directory** | Root container for the skill | `.agents/skills/\u003cskill-name\u003e/` [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-18]() |\n| **`SKILL.md`** | Core definition file | Must be in the skill directory root [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-18]() |\n| **Frontmatter** | Metadata for discovery | YAML block at the top of `SKILL.md` [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:21-24]() |\n| **Body** | Step-by-step instructions | Markdown content below frontmatter [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:26-39]() |\n| **Trigger** | User prompt matching | Controlled by the `description` field [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:44-44]() |\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-45]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"24:T1a07,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Best Practices\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx](docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx](docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx](docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx)\n- [docs/specification.mdx](docs/specification.mdx)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page documents authoring best practices for creating high-quality Agent Skills. It focuses on technical strategies for grounding skills in real expertise, managing agent context efficiently, and implementing iterative loops to ensure reliability.\n\n## Core Philosophy: Expertise over Generality\n\nA common pitfall is relying on an LLM's general training data to generate a skill. This results in vague instructions that fail to capture the nuances of specific APIs, project conventions, or edge cases [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:7-11](). Effective skills are synthesized from domain-specific context.\n\n### Data Synthesis Procedures\nTo create a high-value skill, authors should follow these procedures:\n\n1.  **Extract from Hands-on Tasks**: Perform the task manually in conversation with an agent. Note the specific sequence of actions, corrections made to the agent's initial approach, and the exact input/output formats encountered [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:13-21]().\n2.  **Synthesize from Project Artifacts**: Feed existing technical debt or knowledge into the skill. High-quality sources include internal runbooks, API schemas, version control history (specifically patches/fixes), and real-world failure cases [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:22-33]().\n\n### Skill Refinement Loop\nThe following diagram illustrates the recommended \"Plan-Validate-Execute\" loop for skill development, bridging the gap between natural language requirements and code execution.\n\n**Skill Iteration Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"NaturalLanguageSpace\"\n        A[\"User Intent / Expertise\"] --\u003e B[\"Draft SKILL.md\"]\n        B --\u003e C[\"Trigger Eval Queries\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"CodeEntitySpace\"\n        C --\u003e D[\"Agent Client (e.g. Claude Code)\"]\n        D --\u003e E[\"Discovery: read-properties\"]\n        E --\u003e F[\"Activation: to-prompt\"]\n        F --\u003e G[\"Execution: bash/python scripts/\"]\n    end\n\n    G --\u003e H[\"evals/evals.json (Assertions)\"]\n    H --\u003e I[\"benchmark.json (Results)\"]\n    I --\u003e|Refine Instructions| B\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:34-44](), [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx:107-115](), [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:11-18]()\n\n## Context Parsimony\n\nOnce a skill activates, its full body is loaded into the agent's context window. Every token in `SKILL.md` competes for the agent's attention [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:46-49]().\n\n### The \"Omit what it knows\" Rule\nDo not explain general concepts (e.g., how HTTP works or what a PDF is). Focus exclusively on project-specific conventions and non-obvious procedures [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:50-53]().\n\n| Bad (Verbose) | Good (Parsimonious) |\n| :--- | :--- |\n| \"PDFs are a common format containing text...\" | \"Use pdfplumber for text extraction.\" |\n| \"Handle errors by checking status codes...\" | \"If API returns 429, retry with exponential backoff.\" |\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:54-77]()\n\n### Progressive Disclosure Implementation\nFor skills requiring extensive reference material, use a tiered loading strategy. Keep the core `SKILL.md` under 500 lines and move details to the `references/` directory [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:86-91]().\n\n**Context Loading Architecture**\n```mermaid\nsequenceDiagram\n    participant Agent\n    participant FS as \"Filesystem (.agents/skills/)\"\n    \n    Note over Agent, FS: Discovery Phase\n    Agent-\u003e\u003eFS: Scan \"SKILL.md\" frontmatter (name, description)\n    \n    Note over Agent, FS: Activation Phase (Triggered)\n    Agent-\u003e\u003eFS: Read full \"SKILL.md\" body\n    \n    Note over Agent, FS: Execution Phase (Conditional)\n    Agent-\u003e\u003eAgent: Follows instruction: \"Read references/api-errors.md\"\n    Agent-\u003e\u003eFS: Read \"references/api-errors.md\"\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:90-91](), [docs/specification.mdx:214-223](), [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx:18-31]()\n\n## Calibrating Control\n\nMatch the specificity of instructions to the fragility of the task. \n\n*   **Freedom**: Use descriptive language (\"Check for SQL injection\") when multiple approaches are valid and the task tolerates variation [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:96-107]().\n*   **Prescriptive**: Use strict, stepwise instructions and code blocks when operations are fragile or a specific sequence must be followed [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:109-110]().\n\n### Agentic Script Design\nWhen bundling scripts in `scripts/`, follow these practices:\n*   **Self-Containment**: Scripts should be self-contained or clearly document dependencies. Use PEP 723 for Python or `npx`/`deno` for JavaScript to handle dependencies automatically [docs/specification.mdx:191-197]().\n*   **Relative Pathing**: Always reference scripts using relative paths from the skill root (e.g., `scripts/extract.py`). The agent resolves these relative to the `SKILL.md` location [docs/specification.mdx:226-235]().\n\n## Evaluation and Validation\n\nReliability is achieved through structured evaluations and description optimization.\n\n### Designing Test Cases\nA robust evaluation suite should include:\n*   **Should-trigger queries**: Test if the `description` field in the YAML frontmatter correctly activates the skill for relevant tasks [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:41-50]().\n*   **Near-miss queries**: Queries that share keywords (e.g., \"CSV\") but require different tools (e.g., \"Postgres\"), testing the precision of the description [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:53-65]().\n*   **Trigger Rate**: Run queries multiple times (e.g., 3 runs) to account for LLM non-determinism and calculate a success percentage [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:85-91]().\n\n### Benchmarking ROI\nRun test cases twice: once with the skill and once without (baseline). Record the following to ensure the skill provides a positive ROI on context and time:\n*   `total_tokens`: Total context consumed.\n*   `duration_ms`: Time to completion.\n*   `assertion_passed`: Whether specific quality checks were met.\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:1-18](), [docs/specification.mdx:238-242]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"25:T1df7,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Optimizing Descriptions\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx](docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx](docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx](docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx)\n- [docs/specification.mdx](docs/specification.mdx)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page explains how to craft and refine the `description` field in a `SKILL.md` file to ensure skills trigger reliably. It covers the technical mechanics of skill activation, the methodology for testing trigger rates, and iterative refinement using \"near-miss\" test cases.\n\n## How Skill Triggering Works\n\nThe Agent Skills framework utilizes a **progressive disclosure** model to manage the agent's context window efficiently [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx:18-20]().\n\n1.  **Discovery**: At session startup, the agent scans registered skill directories [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx:32-34](). It parses the YAML frontmatter of each `SKILL.md` file, extracting specifically the `name` and `description` fields [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx:109-112]().\n2.  **Activation**: The agent compares the user's prompt against the available descriptions. At this stage, only the `name` and `description` (approx. 100 tokens) are in context [docs/specification.mdx:218-218](). If a match is found, the agent \"activates\" the skill by loading the full Markdown body (Instructions) into the active context [docs/specification.mdx:219-219]().\n3.  **Execution**: The agent follows the detailed instructions provided in the body and may load additional **Resources** (scripts, references, assets) as needed [docs/specification.mdx:220-220]().\n\nBecause the full instructions are hidden until activation, the `description` field carries the entire burden of triggering the skill [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:15-15]().\n\n### Triggering Data Flow\n\nThe following diagram illustrates how the description acts as the gatekeeper between the discovery phase and the execution phase within the agent's logic.\n\n**Natural Language Space to Code Entity Space: Skill Activation**\n```mermaid\nsequenceDiagram\n    participant U as \"User Prompt (Natural Language)\"\n    participant A as \"Agent Client (e.g. Claude Code)\"\n    participant FM as \"SKILL.md Frontmatter (YAML)\"\n    participant BD as \"SKILL.md Body (Markdown)\"\n\n    U-\u003e\u003eA: \"Process this PDF for me\"\n    A-\u003e\u003eFM: \"read_properties() -\u003e SkillProperties\"\n    Note over A,FM: \"Matches 'description' field?\"\n    FM--\u003e\u003eA: \"Match Found\"\n    A-\u003e\u003eBD: \"to_prompt() -\u003e Full Instructions\"\n    BD--\u003e\u003eA: \"Skill Instructions Loaded\"\n    A-\u003e\u003eU: \"Executes task via tool_use\"\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:11-17](), [docs/client-implementation/adding-skills-support.mdx:20-30](), [docs/specification.mdx:214-222]()\n\n## Designing Trigger Eval Queries\n\nTo optimize a description, authors should develop a suite of approximately 20 evaluation queries [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:39-39](). These queries are categorized into two types:\n\n### 1. Should-Trigger Queries\nThese test the breadth and clarity of the description. They should vary by:\n*   **Phrasing**: Mix formal requests with casual language and typos [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:45-45]().\n*   **Explicitness**: Some should name the domain (e.g., \"analyze this CSV\"), while others should only describe the goal (e.g., \"my boss wants a chart from this data file\") [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:46-46]().\n*   **Complexity**: Include single-step tasks and tasks where the skill is just one part of a larger workflow [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:48-48]().\n\n### 2. Should-Not-Trigger Queries (Near-Misses)\nThe most critical tests are **near-misses**—prompts that share keywords with the skill but require a different capability [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:52-54]().\n\n| Skill Type | Near-Miss Example | Why it shouldn't trigger |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **CSV Analysis** | \"Update formulas in my Excel budget\" | Needs Excel editing, not CSV processing [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:63-63]() |\n| **CSV Analysis** | \"Write a script to upload CSV to Postgres\" | Needs Database ETL, not analysis [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:64-64]() |\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:28-66]()\n\n## Testing and Iteration\n\n### The Trigger Rate Concept\nModel behavior is nondeterministic. A single test run is insufficient to determine if a description is well-calibrated. Authors should run each query multiple times (e.g., 3 runs) to calculate a **trigger rate** [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:87-88]().\n\n*   **Target for Should-Trigger**: Rate \u003e 0.5 [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:89-89]().\n*   **Target for Should-Not-Trigger**: Rate \u003c 0.5 [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:89-90]().\n\n### Automated Testing Logic\nThe following diagram represents the logic for a testing script that evaluates description performance by interacting with an agent's tool-use logs or output transcripts.\n\n**Trigger Rate Evaluation Logic**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    Start[\"Start Eval Loop\"] --\u003e Load[\"Load eval_queries.json\"]\n    Load --\u003e Loop[\"For each Query in Queries\"]\n    Loop --\u003e SubLoop[\"Repeat N times (e.g., 3)\"]\n    SubLoop --\u003e Invoke[\"Invoke Agent (e.g. via CLI)\"]\n    Invoke --\u003e Check[\"Check tool_use/logs for Skill activation\"]\n    Check --\u003e Result{\"Skill Triggered?\"}\n    Result -- \"Yes\" --\u003e Inc[\"Increment Trigger Count\"]\n    Result -- \"No\" --\u003e Next[\"Next Run\"]\n    Inc --\u003e Next\n    Next --\u003e EndSub{\"Finished N runs?\"}\n    EndSub -- \"No\" --\u003e SubLoop\n    EndSub -- \"Yes\" --\u003e Calc[\"Calculate Trigger Rate (Count / N)\"]\n    Calc --\u003e Validate{\"Rate matches should_trigger?\"}\n    Validate -- \"Fail\" --\u003e Refine[\"Refine 'description' in SKILL.md\"]\n    Validate -- \"Pass\" --\u003e NextQuery[\"Move to next Query\"]\n    Refine --\u003e Start\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:85-110]()\n\n## Refinement Best Practices\n\nWhen a skill fails to trigger or triggers too often, refine the `description` field using these principles:\n\n*   **Imperative Phrasing**: Use \"Use this skill when...\" to provide a direct instruction to the agent [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:23-23]().\n*   **Intent Over Mechanics**: Describe the user's goal (e.g., \"extract text from PDFs\") rather than the internal implementation (e.g., \"runs pdfplumber\") [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:24-24]().\n*   **Explicit Contexts**: List specific scenarios, even if the user might not use technical terms (e.g., \"even if they don't explicitly mention 'CSV'\") [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:25-25]().\n*   **Conciseness**: Keep the description under the 1024-character limit to avoid context bloat [docs/specification.mdx:94-94]().\n*   **Fragility Calibration**: For fragile tasks, be more prescriptive in the description about when the skill is the *only* valid path [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:109-109]().\n*   **Avoid Redundancy**: Do not explain general concepts the agent already knows (e.g., what a PDF is); focus on the unique value the skill adds [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:50-52]().\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/optimizing-descriptions.mdx:19-27](), [docs/skill-creation/best-practices.mdx:50-53](), [docs/specification.mdx:91-108]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"26:T2126,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Evaluating Skills\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/clients.mdx](docs/clients.mdx)\n- [docs/docs.json](docs/docs.json)\n- [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx](docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx](docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx](docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx)\n- [docs/snippets/ClientShowcase.jsx](docs/snippets/ClientShowcase.jsx)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nEvaluating a skill is the process of systematically testing whether it produces high-quality, reliable outputs across varied prompts and edge cases. This framework moves beyond \"vibes-based\" testing to an engineering-driven feedback loop involving test cases, assertion-based grading, and baseline comparisons.\n\n## The Evaluation Framework\n\nThe evaluation system is designed to compare the performance of an agent **with** a skill against its performance **without** that skill (the baseline). This ensures that the skill is actually providing value and not just consuming context tokens.\n\n### Workspace Structure\n\nEvaluation results are organized into a workspace directory separate from the skill itself. This prevents test artifacts from polluting the skill's distribution.\n\n```text\ncsv-analyzer/\n├── SKILL.md\n└── evals/\n    └── evals.json             # Source of truth for test cases\ncsv-analyzer-workspace/\n└── iteration-1/               # A single pass of the eval loop\n    ├── eval-case-id/          # Results for a specific test case\n    │   ├── with_skill/        # Run with the skill active\n    │   │   ├── outputs/       # Files produced by the agent\n    │   │   ├── timing.json    # Token usage and duration\n    │   │   └── grading.json   # Results of assertions\n    │   └── without_skill/     # Baseline run (no skill)\n    │       ├── outputs/\n    │       ├── timing.json\n    │       └── grading.json\n    └── benchmark.json         # Aggregated stats for the iteration\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:52-82]()\n\n## Test Case Definition (`evals.json`)\n\nTest cases are stored in the `evals/evals.json` file within the skill directory. Each case defines the input environment and the criteria for success.\n\n| Field | Type | Description |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `skill_name` | `string` | The identifier of the skill being tested. |\n| `id` | `integer` | A unique identifier for the test case. |\n| `prompt` | `string` | The realistic user message to send to the agent. |\n| `expected_output` | `string` | A human-readable description of success. |\n| `files` | `array` | Paths to mock data or input files needed for the task. |\n| `assertions` | `array` | (Optional) List of verifiable statements for grading. |\n\n### Example Structure\n```json\n{\n  \"skill_name\": \"csv-analyzer\",\n  \"evals\": [\n    {\n      \"id\": 1,\n      \"prompt\": \"Find the top 3 months by revenue in data/sales_2025.csv.\",\n      \"expected_output\": \"A bar chart image with labeled axes.\",\n      \"files\": [\"evals/files/sales_2025.csv\"],\n      \"assertions\": [\n        \"The output file is a valid PNG\",\n        \"The axes are labeled 'Month' and 'Revenue'\"\n      ]\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:19-37](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:146-155]()\n\n## Execution and Data Flow\n\nThe evaluation process follows a strict flow to ensure clean context and reproducible results. Each run starts with a fresh session to isolate the agent's behavior to the provided `SKILL.md` instructions.\n\nTitle: Skill Evaluation Data Flow\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Natural Language Space\"\n        UserPrompt[\"'Analyze this CSV'\"]\n        SuccessCriteria[\"'Valid JSON output'\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Code Entity Space (Files \u0026 Structures)\"\n        EvalsJSON[\"evals/evals.json\"]\n        SkillMD[\"SKILL.md\"]\n        TimingJSON[\"timing.json\"]\n        GradingJSON[\"grading.json\"]\n        BenchmarkJSON[\"benchmark.json\"]\n    end\n\n    UserPrompt --\u003e|\"stored in 'prompt' field\"| EvalsJSON\n    SuccessCriteria --\u003e|\"stored in 'assertions' array\"| EvalsJSON\n    SkillMD --\u003e|\"loaded into context\"| AgentRun[\"Agent Execution\"]\n    EvalsJSON --\u003e|\"provides input\"| AgentRun\n    AgentRun --\u003e|\"emits stats\"| TimingJSON\n    AgentRun --\u003e|\"produces files\"| GradingJSON\n    TimingJSON --\u003e|\"aggregated into\"| BenchmarkJSON\n    GradingJSON --\u003e|\"aggregated into\"| BenchmarkJSON\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:19-37](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:88-95](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:116-121](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:163-171]()\n\n## Assertion-Based Grading\n\nAssertions are objective, verifiable statements used to grade the agent's performance. They are typically added after an initial review of the first run's output to establish a standard for \"good\" performance.\n\n1.  **Objective Checks**: Programmatic verification (e.g., \"Output is valid JSON\").\n2.  **Observable Checks**: Specific UI or content elements (e.g., \"The chart has labeled axes\").\n3.  **Grading**: Each assertion is marked as `true` (pass) or `false` (fail).\n\n### Output Format (`grading.json`)\nThe results of these checks are persisted in a `grading.json` file for each run.\n```json\n{\n  \"assertions\": [\n    {\n      \"assertion\": \"The output file is a valid PNG\",\n      \"passed\": true,\n      \"reason\": \"File exists at outputs/chart.png and has correct magic bytes.\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:127-142](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:163-171]()\n\n## Performance Metrics\n\nBeyond correctness, evaluations track the \"cost\" of a skill in terms of latency and token consumption. This allows authors to identify if a skill adds excessive overhead.\n\n### Execution Transcripts and Timing\nEvery run generates a `timing.json` file. This data is often captured from agent notifications (e.g., Claude Code task completion messages).\n\n| Metric | Key in `timing.json` | Source |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Token Usage** | `total_tokens` | Agent API / Task Completion |\n| **Latency** | `duration_ms` | Wall clock time of the run |\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:116-121](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:123-125]()\n\n## Blind Comparison\n\nTo eliminate bias during human review, the framework supports \"blind\" comparison. A reviewer is presented with two outputs (Output A and Output B) without knowing which one was produced with the skill and which was the baseline.\n\n### Comparison Workflow\n1.  **Snapshot**: The reviewer looks at `with_skill/outputs` and `without_skill/outputs`.\n2.  **Selection**: The reviewer chooses the better output based on the `expected_output` criteria.\n3.  **Record**: The preference is recorded in the iteration's `benchmark.json`.\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:173-195]()\n\n## Aggregation (`benchmark.json`)\n\nAt the end of an evaluation iteration, all data is aggregated into a single `benchmark.json` file. This file provides the high-level metrics used to decide if a skill is ready for release.\n\nTitle: Evaluation Result Aggregation\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Per-Run Entities\"\n        T_WS[\"with_skill/timing.json\"]\n        G_WS[\"with_skill/grading.json\"]\n        T_WOS[\"without_skill/timing.json\"]\n        G_WOS[\"without_skill/grading.json\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Summary Entity\"\n        B[\"benchmark.json\"]\n    end\n\n    T_WS --\u003e|\"sums tokens\"| B\n    T_WOS --\u003e|\"sums tokens\"| B\n    G_WS --\u003e|\"calculates pass rate\"| B\n    G_WOS --\u003e|\"calculates pass rate\"| B\n    B --\u003e|\"outputs\"| FinalReport[\"Final Pass/Fail %\"]\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:81-84](), [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx:163-171]()\n\n## Iteration Loop\n\nThe evaluation process is iterative, using the results of one run to refine the skill instructions or description.\n\n1.  **Draft**: Write the `SKILL.md`.\n2.  **Define**: Create `evals.json` with prompts and files.\n3.  **Execute**: Run the agent with and without the skill.\n4.  **Grade**: Add assertions to `evals.json` and fill `grading.json`.\n5.  **Analyze**: Review `benchmark.json` for regressions or token bloat.\n6.  **Refine**: Update `SKILL.md` or the `description` and repeat.\n\nSources: Using Scripts\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/clients.mdx](docs/clients.mdx)\n- [docs/docs.json](docs/docs.json)\n- [docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx](docs/skill-creation/evaluating-skills.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx](docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx)\n- [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx](docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx)\n- [docs/snippets/ClientShowcase.jsx](docs/snippets/ClientShowcase.jsx)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page provides a technical guide for adding executable scripts to Agent Skills. It covers supported runtimes, dependency management strategies, and architectural principles for designing scripts that are effectively utilized by AI agents.\n\n## Overview\n\nWhile the `SKILL.md` file provides the primary instructions for an agent, complex logic, specialized data processing, or fragile multi-step operations are often better handled by executable scripts [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:7-8](). Scripts allow skill authors to bundle reusable logic within a `scripts/` directory, ensuring that the agent has access to reliable, tested tools rather than relying solely on generated code [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:87-88]().\n\n## Script Invocation Flow\n\nThe following diagram illustrates how an agent transitions from natural language instructions in `SKILL.md` to executing a concrete script entity within the skill's filesystem.\n\n**Natural Language to Script Execution Bridge**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"NaturalLanguageSpace\"\n        A[\"Skill Instructions\"] -- \"References\" --\u003e B[\"## Available scripts\"]\n        B -- \"Describes\" --\u003e C[\"scripts/process.py\"]\n        D[\"## Workflow\"] -- \"Instructs execution\" --\u003e E[\"bash scripts/validate.sh\"]\n    }\n\n    subgraph \"CodeEntitySpace\"\n        F[\"/.agents/skills/my-skill/\"]\n        G[\"SKILL.md\"]\n        H[\"scripts/\"]\n        I[\"scripts/process.py\"]\n        J[\"scripts/validate.sh\"]\n        \n        F --\u003e G\n        F --\u003e H\n        H --\u003e I\n        H --\u003e J\n    }\n\n    C -.-\u003e I\n    E -.-\u003e J\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:93-116](), [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:18-24]()\n\n## Supported Runtimes and Dependency Management\n\nAgent Skills support various runtimes. The preferred approach is using \"self-contained scripts\" that declare their own dependencies inline, eliminating the need for complex environment setup or separate manifest files [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:122-124]().\n\n### Python (PEP 723 / uv)\nPython scripts can use [PEP 723](https://peps.python.org/pep-0723/) metadata to define dependencies in a TOML block [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:130-132]().\n*   **Execution:** `uv run scripts/script.py` is recommended as it creates isolated environments and handles caching [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:145-149]().\n*   **Alternative:** `pipx run` also supports PEP 723 [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:151-151]().\n\n### Node.js / TypeScript (npx / Deno / Bun)\n*   **npx:** Runs npm packages on demand, bundled with Node.js [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:37-44]().\n*   **Deno:** Uses `npm:` and `jsr:` import specifiers for self-contained scripts [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:158-160]().\n*   **Bun:** Automatically installs missing packages at runtime if `node_modules` is absent [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:180-181]().\n\n### One-off Commands\nFor simple tasks, agents can invoke tools directly using ecosystem-specific runners:\n| Tool | Runtime | Description |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `uvx` | Python | Runs packages in isolated, cached environments [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:15-18](). |\n| `npx` | Node.js | Standard tool for running npm packages without local install [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:37-40](). |\n| `go run` | Go | Compiles and runs Go packages directly [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:71-74](). |\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:11-81](), [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:128-181]()\n\n## Path Resolution and References\n\nAgents resolve script paths relative to the **skill directory root** [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:91-92](). This applies both to the `SKILL.md` instructions and any supporting documentation in `references/` [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:118-120]().\n\n**Path Resolution Logic**\n```mermaid\nflowchart LR\n    subgraph \"AgentRuntime\"\n        direction TB\n        K[\"Working Directory\"]\n        L[\"Skill Root Path\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"SkillDirectoryStructure\"\n        M[\"SKILL.md\"]\n        N[\"scripts/task.py\"]\n    end\n\n    K -- \"Resolves\" --\u003e L\n    L -- \"Relative Reference\" --\u003e N\n    M -- \"Code Block: python3 scripts/task.py\" --\u003e N\n```\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:89-120]()\n\n## Agentic Script Design Principles\n\nWhen writing scripts for agents, authors should follow \"agentic\" design principles to ensure reliability and observability.\n\n1.  **Match Specificity to Fragility:** Use prescriptive scripts for fragile operations where consistency and exact sequences matter [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:87-88]().\n2.  **Explicit Prerequisites:** State runtime requirements (e.g., \"Requires Node.js 18+\") in the `SKILL.md` or use the `compatibility` frontmatter field [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:86-86]().\n3.  **Version Pinning:** Always pin versions (e.g., `npx eslint@9.0.0`) to ensure reproducible behavior across different agent environments [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:85-85]().\n4.  **Complex Logic Encapsulation:** Move complex commands into scripts when they grow hard to get right on the first try via raw terminal instructions [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:87-88]().\n\n### Progressive Disclosure for Scripts\nFor large skills, use scripts and reference files to implement progressive disclosure. Instead of bloating the `SKILL.md` with every instruction, provide high-level metadata and instruct the agent to run specific scripts or read specific files only when the skill is activated for a relevant task [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:63-71]().\n\nSources: [docs/skill-creation/using-scripts.mdx:83-88](), [docs/skill-creation/quickstart.mdx:61-71]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"28:T26f6,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# skills-ref Reference Library\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [skills-ref/README.md](skills-ref/README.md)\n- [skills-ref/src/skills_ref/__init__.py](skills-ref/src/skills_ref/__init__.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\n## Purpose and Scope\n\nThe `skills-ref` reference library is a Python-based implementation demonstrating how to work with the Agent Skills format. It provides validation, metadata extraction, and prompt generation capabilities for `SKILL.md` files. **This library is intended for demonstration purposes only and is not designed for production use** [skills-ref/README.md:5-6]().\n\nThis page provides an architectural overview of the `skills-ref` library, its core capabilities, and how it processes Agent Skills. For detailed information on specific aspects:\n- Installation procedures, see [Installation and Setup](#5.1)\n- CLI command usage, see [Command-Line Interface](#5.2)\n- Python API functions, see [Python API](#5.3)\n- Contributing to development, see [Development Guide](#5.4)\n- Project dependencies and configuration, see [Dependencies and Configuration](#5.5)\n\nFor information on the `SKILL.md` format that this library processes, see [SKILL.md Specification](#2.2).\n\n## Role in the Agent Skills Ecosystem\n\nThe `skills-ref` library serves as a reference implementation for two primary audiences:\n\n1. **Skill Authors**: Validates `SKILL.md` files during development to ensure conformance with the specification.\n2. **Agent Developers**: Demonstrates how to integrate Agent Skills support, including metadata extraction and prompt generation.\n\n### Ecosystem Position\n\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    SkillAuthor[\"Skill Author\"]\n    SkillDirectory[\"Skill Directory\u003cbr/\u003econtaining SKILL.md\"]\n    \n    subgraph \"skills-ref Library\"\n        Validate[\"validate()\u003cbr/\u003e[src/skills_ref/validator.py]\"]\n Problems\u003cbr/\u003eList[str]\"]\n    SkillProps[\"SkillProperties\u003cbr/\u003eObject\"]\n    XMLPrompt[\"\u003cavailable_skills\u003e\u003cbr/\u003eXML Block\"]\n    \n    AgentDeveloper[\"Agent Developer\"]\n    AgentSystem[\"Agent System\u003cbr/\u003eImplementation\"]\n    SystemPrompt[\"Agent System Prompt\u003cbr/\u003ewith Skill Metadata\"]\n    \n    SkillAuthor --\u003e SkillDirectory\n    SkillDirectory --\u003e Validate\n    Validate --\u003e ValidationReport\n    ValidationReport --\u003e SkillAuthor\n    \n    SkillDirectory --\u003e ReadProps\n    ReadProps --\u003e SkillProps\n    \n    SkillDirectory --\u003e ToPrompt\n    ToPrompt --\u003e XMLPrompt\n    \n    AgentDeveloper --\u003e ToPrompt\n    XMLPrompt --\u003e SystemPrompt\n    SystemPrompt --\u003e AgentSystem\n```\n\n**Sources:** [skills-ref/README.md:1-113](), [skills-ref/src/skills_ref/__init__.py:3-18]()\n\n## Core Capabilities\n\nThe `skills-ref` library exposes three primary functions through both CLI and Python API interfaces:\n\n| Capability | CLI Command | Python Function | Purpose |\n|------------|-------------|-----------------|---------|\n| **Validation** | `skills-ref validate` | `validate()` | Checks `SKILL.md` conformance to specification |\n| **Property Extraction** | `skills-ref read-properties` | `read_properties()` | Extracts metadata from `SKILL.md` frontmatter |\n| **Prompt Generation** | `skills-ref to-prompt` | `to_prompt()` | Generates XML blocks for agent system prompts |\n\nEach capability operates on filesystem paths pointing to skill directories containing `SKILL.md` files [skills-ref/README.md:58-67]().\n\n## Command and Function Flow\n\n### Validation Flow\n\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    SkillPath[\"Path to Skill\u003cbr/\u003eDirectory\"]\n    \n    subgraph \"CLI Interface\"\n        ValidateCLI[\"skills-ref validate\"]\n    end\n    \n    subgraph \"Python API [src/skills_ref/validator.py]\"\n Required Fields\"]\n    end\n    \n    Problems[\"List[str]\u003cbr/\u003eValidation Problems\"]\n    Success[\"Exit Code 0\"]\n    \n    SkillPath --\u003e ValidateCLI\n    SkillPath --\u003e ValidateFunc\n    \n    ValidateCLI --\u003e ValidateFunc\n    ValidateFunc --\u003e LoadSKILL\n    LoadSKILL --\u003e ParseYAML\n    ParseYAML --\u003e CheckSchema\n    \n    CheckSchema --\u003e Problems\n    CheckSchema --\u003e Success\n```\n\n**Sources:** [skills-ref/src/skills_ref/validator.py:7-7](), [skills-ref/README.md:58-60]()\n\n### Property Extraction and Prompt Generation Flow\n\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    SkillPathA[\"Path(/skill-a)\"]\n    SkillPathB[\"Path(/skill-b)\"]\n    \n    subgraph \"read-properties [src/skills_ref/parser.py]\"\n to-prompt\"]\n        ToPromptFunc[\"to_prompt()\"]\n        AvailableSkills[\"XML String Output\"]\n    end\n    \n    SkillPathA --\u003e ReadPropsCLI\n    ReadPropsCLI --\u003e ReadPropsFunc\n    Skill[\"\u003cskill\u003e\"]\n        Name[\"\u003cname\u003e\"]\n        Desc[\"\u003cdescription\u003e\"]\n        Loc[\"\u003clocation\u003e\"]\n    end\n    \n    AvailableSkills --\u003e
